---
title: "Portable Polarization: Partisan Identity, Expressive Attitudes, and COVID-19 Health Behavior"
# Running head: Portable Polarization in Health Behavior (42 characters)
# Word count: auto-calculated at render (see word-count chunk)
# Tables: 1
# Figures: 5
date: today
abstract: |
  Partisan differences in COVID-19 social contacts and mask usage varied over time and across geographic contexts, yet no standard competing account fully explained them. Using six waves of the nationally representative Berkeley Interpersonal Contact Study (BICS; N = 18,233; April 2020–May 2021), we find that partisan identity independently predicts both behaviors after accounting for demographics, policy environments, regional context, and local conditions, a pattern we term "portable polarization": the gap varies across contexts but never disappears. The partisan gap in mask-wearing exceeded differences by race, gender, or education. Gaps narrowed in theoretically revealing ways: rising mortality temporarily compressed the contact gap and the mask gap narrowed among the most concerned, but none of these factors eliminated the partisan association. Partisans in opposing-party districts adjusted modestly toward local norms without closing the divide. These patterns converge on social observability as the channel through which partisan identity is associated with health behavior: the private behavior tracked objective risk conditions while the visible behavior functioned as a publicly legible identity marker, its expression governed more by social norms and audience composition than by health risk. Because BICS captures actual daily contact counts and mask usage rates rather than abstract behavioral intentions, these findings also provide direct estimates of partisan heterogeneity relevant to disease transmission modeling.
keywords:
  - COVID-19
  - political polarization
  - health behaviors
  - partisanship
  - public health
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: letter
    fontsize: 12pt
    mainfont: "Times New Roman"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    linestretch: 2
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
csl: american-sociological-association.csl
linestretch: 2
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: sentence
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center"
)
```

```{r word-count}
#| label: word-count
#| include: false

# ── Word count utility ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Counts prose words in .qmd files, stripping:
#   - YAML front matter (--- ... ---)
#   - Code chunks (```{...} ... ```)
#   - HTML comments (<!-- ... -->)
#   - Inline R code (`r ...`)
#   - Markdown headers that are purely structural (# ...)
#   - Blank lines
# JHSB limit: 10,000 words (title + abstract + text + notes + references)

count_prose_words <- function(filepath) {
  if (!file.exists(filepath)) return(0)
  lines <- readLines(filepath, warn = FALSE)
  text <- paste(lines, collapse = "\n")

  # Remove YAML front matter
  text <- gsub("(?s)^---.*?---", "", text, perl = TRUE)
  # Remove code chunks
  text <- gsub("(?s)```\\{.*?```", "", text, perl = TRUE)
  # Remove HTML comments
  text <- gsub("(?s)<!--.*?-->", "", text, perl = TRUE)
  # Remove inline R code
  text <- gsub("`r [^`]+`", "", text)
  # Remove markdown image/link syntax artifacts
  text <- gsub("\\!?\\[([^]]*)\\]\\([^)]*\\)", "\\1", text)
  # Remove remaining markdown formatting
  text <- gsub("[#*_`>]", "", text)
  # Remove cross-reference labels like {#sec-...} and {.unnumbered}
  text <- gsub("\\{[^}]*\\}", "", text)

  words <- strsplit(trimws(text), "\\s+")[[1]]
  words <- words[nchar(words) > 0]
  length(words)
}

# Count each section
section_dir <- here::here("paper", "jhsb_submission", "sections")
sections <- c(
  "Title + Abstract" = here::here("paper", "jhsb_submission", "partisan_differences.qmd"),
  "Introduction" = file.path(section_dir, "introduction.qmd"),
  "Background" = file.path(section_dir, "background.qmd"),
  "Data and Methods" = file.path(section_dir, "methods.qmd"),
  "Results" = file.path(section_dir, "results.qmd"),
  "Discussion" = file.path(section_dir, "discussion.qmd")
)

word_counts <- sapply(sections, count_prose_words)
text_total <- sum(word_counts)

# Estimate references (will be filled in after first render; use placeholder)
# Typical: 30-50 citations × ~30 words each ≈ 900-1500
ref_estimate <- 1200
grand_total <- text_total + ref_estimate

# Build summary
wc_summary <- tibble(
  Section = c(names(word_counts), "References (est.)", "TOTAL"),
  Words = c(word_counts, ref_estimate, grand_total)
)

# Print to console during render
message("\n═══ JHSB WORD COUNT ═══")
for (i in seq_len(nrow(wc_summary))) {
  message(sprintf("  %-25s %5d", wc_summary$Section[i], wc_summary$Words[i]))
}
message(sprintf("\n  JHSB limit: 10,000 words"))
message(sprintf("  Headroom:   %s words", format(10000 - grand_total, big.mark = ",")))
message("═══════════════════════\n")
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Manuscript word count:** `r format(grand_total, big.mark = ",")` (text: `r format(text_total, big.mark = ",")`; references est.: `r format(ref_estimate, big.mark = ",")`) | Tables: 1 | Figures: 5 | Running head: Partisan Differences in Health Behaviors
:::

{{< include sections/introduction.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/background.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/methods.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/results.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/discussion.qmd >}}

\newpage

# References

::: {#refs}
:::

{{< include sections/appendix.qmd >}}
